Powering Persuasion
with Creativity
How to Create Ideas, Words and Images
That Persuade
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 9 --

Whether you’re a marketer, a copywriter, a salesperson or just want to persuade your spouse
to watch the Netflix series that you want to watch for a change, here are endless ways to apply
the 15 thinking triggers (boxes) from my Idea Power training to your persuasive endeavors.
What follows merely scratches the surface. But I hope it will set you on the right path and get
you familiar with using the boxes.
Box 1: Add a New Dimension
A powerful way to persuade someone to do something is to help them picture the benefits
(adding a visual dimension).
More powerful still is to help them picture themselves enjoying the benefits—bringing the
concept to life with sensory and emotional details that make it more vivid and far more
persuasive.
Don’t just say, “Let’s eat Thai food tonight.” Talk about how much they’re going to enjoy those
summer rolls drenched in peanut sauce.
Same goes for anything you want to persuade people to do, in speaking, writing or marketing—
paint a picture.
Give People What They Want
Ted Nicholas had a very successful headline for a course on public speaking that brought in the
dimensions of sight and sound—as well as a deep emotional feeling of satisfaction: “How to Get
Standing Ovation… Every Time You Speak!” So simple, vivid and persuasive.
Box 2: Do the Opposite
Doing the opposite may well be the oldest trick in the persuasion handbook.
Like when you use negative psychology on a child and tell them to do the opposite of what you
want them to do. (“You’d better not put that toy away!”)
Now, I’m not saying you should literally tell others to do the opposite of what you want them to
do.
But going to the opposite of what is expected is often a very powerful way to get attention and
differentiate yourself. For example, Tesla does many things the opposite to traditional car
manufacturers, such as not selling through dealers and not negotiating on price.
What’s Good for You Is Bad
Arthur Johnson’s copy to promote a health newsletter exploited the power of the opposite by
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 9 --

focusing on how many things you might think are good for you that are actually bad for you,
and many things you think are bad for that you are actually healthy. What a great way to get
the attention of so many people who are fed up with being constantly misled.
Box 3: Divide It Up
So many sales pitches, ads, sales pages and emails try to cram in too much.
The prospect gets overwhelmed.
Better to divide it up and keep everything simple and centered around a central theme.
So for example, perhaps that ad or sales page should really be two (or more) different ads or
sales pages.
I’ve often been tempted to take an ad that gave five reasons to buy a product, and instead
divided it into five smaller ads, each with a single reason.
TL;DR: Divide and conquer.
How To Make Money Giving Cars Away
In the 1920s, General Motors had trouble competing against low-priced Ford Model Ts. They
couldn’t lower their prices. So they promoted that their cars were free to drive away—by
dividing up the price and pioneering selling cars on credit with installment payments. Sure,
installment plans are old hat these days. But can you divide up the way your prospects pay in
other ways, such as by dividing up the product or service and selling the different components
separately? Or bundling together?
Box 4: Take Something Out
Sometimes, what you take out of an argument or sales message can be as important to
persuasion as what you leave in.
You want to eliminate whatever is hard to believe, boring or irrelevant to your point.
Taking something out can also mean highlighting it. Sometimes, we want to make very general
claims and arguments. But what often persuades people most is specifics.
The Power of Single Focus
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 9 --

There are many examples of great ads highlighting one particular aspect of a product or service.
One of the best known, perhaps, is the Ogilvy ad for Rolls Royce: “At 60 Miles per Hour, the
Loudest Noise in This New Rolls Royce Comes From the Electric Clock.”
Box 5: Bring Some Things Together
A good way to get attention is to merge current events, celebrities or even familiar book or
movie titles into your sales pitch or promotion. Or even the name of your product.
You see this often in the diet industry: The South Beach Diet, celebrity endorsements and the
attempt to associate the diet with celebrities (“all the celebrities are doing it”).
There’s a Great Idea Born Every Minute
When my friend Joe Vitale challenged me to come up with a title for his great book on the
marketing secrets of P.T. Barnum, I merged it with a saying Barnum is known for and came up
with: There’s a Customer Born Every Minute. Then, to promote a book on estate planning, I
played off what, at the time, was the title of a popular book and movie: What Every Spouse
Wishes Their Partner Knew About Estate Planning (But the IRS Hopes You Never Find Out).
Box #6: Put One Thing Inside Another
In persuasion, advertising and promotion, so many opportunities are found when you think
about putting something inside something else.
Testimonials in a sales pitch. Advertising inserts inside a newsletter mailing. Sidebars in print
ads and sales letters. A survey on your website to increase traffic and engagement.
Suddenly, What’s Old Is New Again
One of my favorite examples of this is to take an entire mailing piece and re-mail it, envelope
and all, in a slightly larger envelope with a note that says: “This is important—and so many
people have already achieved great results from it—that I’m resending it to make sure you
don’t miss out.”
Box #7: Do It Beforehand
In persuasion and advertising, it pays to think about what you can do before your sales pitch to
make prospects more likely to take the action you want them to take. To build trust. Or
awareness. To make them more receptive to your arguments.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 9 --

Good Stuff Is On the Way!
Often I’ll recommend a client do something before a promotional campaign is launched to build
credibility, trust or anticipation. For example, sending a simple postcard or email to prospects
telling them what’s coming and why they should be on the lookout for it, and how it will benefit
them to pay attention to it. This is a great way to increase open rates.
Box #8: Convert Into a Benefit
Turning lemons into lemonade is as old as advertising itself. But it’s not just lemons you want to
squeeze. Ordinary features, with a little work, can become persuasive benefits. I like how my
friend Alan Rospenspan demonstrates this with a pencil: The yellow color makes it easy to find
when you need it. The beveled edges mean it won’t roll away. It’s lightweight, so you won’t get
tired using it. And best of all—it’s made of wood, so it’s biodegradable!
Lemons Into Lemonade
In the 1960s, advertising for the Volkswagen Beetle (“The Bug”) made its small size a virtue with
a classic ad headlined: “Think Small.” They even had one simply titled “Lemon,” which turned
into a virtue the fact that inspectors occasionally found (and rejected) cars that didn’t pass
muster.
Box #9: Switch Gears
In persuasion, you should always be switching gears.
Because it gets and keeps people interested. It’s boring when an ad or sales presentation lacks
variety.
But you should also think about switching gears in the sense of trying completely different
things. If you’ve got a promotional campaign that’s serious, try something humorous. If it’s full
of hype, try focusing on building trust instead. If your advertising is calm and reasonable, try
adding vibrancy and excitement.
This is where the big breakthroughs come from.
“Step Right Up, Folks…”
If you want to learn how to sell, study pitchmen—at state fairs, on TV infomercials. (You can
find plenty of videos on YouTube.) They have to be good at attracting attention and selling.
Notice how they often do it by switching gears: from almost excited shouting to a calm whisper,
from funny to serious… from hyperbole to understatement.
Box #10: Revise the Rhythm
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 9 --

Everything has a rhythm. Especially advertising.
There’s a rhythm to how often you make a promise or add proof to your copy.
There’s a rhythm to how often you run your advertising.
Find the habitual rhythms and vary them. Try more often. Less often. More predictably. Less
predictably.
(See how I revised the rhythm in this section?)
I Got Rhythm
When sending emails to a list, one of the best things you can do is revise the rhythm of when
and how often you send. Most people find two things when they do this: 1) Recipients like a
predictable rhythm of getting your emails, and 2) sending more often than you think you should
is often more profitable than expected and bothers recipients less than expected. (Many people
I work with mail daily to great success.)
Box 11: Do It Itself
In persuasion, the most important “do it itself” is when an argument is presented in such a way
that you are not trying to convince the prospect so much as allowing the prospect to convince
themselves.
Which is ultimately the only way anyone is ever persuaded anyway.
People Love To Tell You About Themselves
Perhaps the best example in advertising of “do it itself” is the survey funnel, where an online
prospect is funneled into a survey on a landing page. The prospect then does the work of
qualifying and categorizing themselves through the answers they give. And if the questions are
phrased correctly, they are even doing some of the work of selling themselves by answering the
questions, because it forces them to consider their pain and possible solutions.
Box #12: Change the Intensity of the Interaction
Today when consumers are so bombarded by sales messages of all types, a change in intensity
is a very useful tool for standing out. Try turning it up with more emotion and sensationalism.
Or dial it down with calm understatement when everyone else is shouting. You can even try
varying it within the same piece to keep prospects riveted.
Whispering and Shouting Both Work
Some of the most successful commercials in history worked because they featured intensity
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 9 --

consumers weren’t accustomed to—from the calm quiet of Ronald Reagan’s hugely successful
“Morning in America” commercials for his re-election campaign to the hyperactive “Crazy
Eddie” retail commercials that ran for years in New York City. Check them out on YouTube.
Box #13: Add Something to the Interaction
When we interact with someone to persuade them, we tend to focus on what we should say.
But the best persuaders also focus on what can be added to the interaction itself, such as
building trust or increasing engagement, both of which often make prospects more responsive.
An important element you can add to virtually any persuasion interaction is to get feedback—
by simply asking for it, including a survey or giving them the opportunity to take a quiz. Not only
will you get valuable data on your customers and prospects, but including a survey or quiz is an
involvement device that can dramatically increase response. (Fundraisers use quizzes, surveys
and other involvement devices in their mailings and online because these tools invariably
increase donations.)
Stock Up on Coffee, My Pretty
I once effectively used Margaret Hamilton, the Wicked Witch of the West, in an ad for Maxwell
House Coffee to retailers.
Ever since, I’ve been constantly on the lookout for ways to add the power of a recognized
celebrity to any ads or promotions I create. And a recognized celebrity doesn’t have to be
expensive. They could be well known only within your industry. And there are plenty of less
popular but still recognizable actors out there, who would welcome spokesperson work.
Box #14: Change the Shape
The shape of your argument matters a lot.
Sometimes a straightforward argument is best.
Sometimes, it’s best if an argument is indirect: starts with something only tangentially related
and eases into the point you want to make.
Consider using lack of balance and asymmetry to get attention and keep readers or viewers on
their toes.
Boost Response Without Changing a Word
In direct mail, I’ve seen that changing the vehicle that delivers the copy can have a big impact
on response. I’ve been able to revitalize many mailings by switching to formats, like from an
envelope mailing to a tabloid-style magazine (or vice versa).
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 9 --

Box #15: Change the Look
In advertising, you change the look and very often you change the result for the better—the
format, the media, the colors, even the typography (increasing font size alone can often
increase response). Changing the look can also refer to how transparent you are in your
advertising. Especially today, honesty and openness (even vulnerability) can form extremely
powerful brand virtues. So, try being more truthful and forthcoming about your product and
sales process. People feel and prefer it.
The Power of Transparency
When I worked at David Ogilvy’s advertising agency on Madison Avenue, I saw what a master
he was at transparency—always up front to clients and staff about his own foibles, failings and
weaknesses. It was an important part of his personal brand and charm. He even titled his
bestselling autobiography: Confessions of an Advertising Man.
As I said in the beginning, this only scratches the surface.
There are an infinite number of ways to apply these boxes.
What is important is not that you do it right, but simply the new thoughts, connections and
ideas the questions trigger in you.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
